prompt: Are the Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture and the Hodge conjecture known to be equivalent, or known to imply each other in either direction? Reply YES or NO, then briefly explain whether the two Millennium problems are known to be linked or independent.

OFF.completion:
The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer) conjecture and the Hodge conjecture are both major unsolved problems in number theory and algebraic geometry, respectively. They are not known to be equivalent or to imply each other in either direction. In fact, they are considered to be distinct problems with different areas of focus.

The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture is about the arithmetic of elliptic curves. It states that the group of rational points on an elliptic curve over the rational numbers is finite if and only if the order of the zero of the L-function of the curve at s=1 is equal to the rank of the curve. This conjecture is a part of the more general ABC conjecture and has been proven in some special cases but remains open in general.

The Hodge conjecture, on the other hand, is about the algebraic cycles on complex projectivevarieties. It states that every Hodge class on a complex projective variety is a linear combination with integer coefficients of classes of algebraic cycles. This conjecture is also a part of the more general Hodge theory and has been proven in some special cases but remains open in general.

While both conjectures are related to the study of algebraic varieties and their arithmetic properties, they deal with different aspects of these objects and do not directly imply each other. Therefore, the answer to the question is:

\boxed{NO}
